title: Political polarization in financial news
abstract: Comparing coverage of the same corporate financial news by the conservative Wall Street Journal and the liberal New York Times, we find strong evidence of political polarization in their reporting on both the intensive and extensive margins of coverage. We show that this politics-induced disagreement in corporate financial news leads to an increase in abnormal trading volume for the most politically extreme firms. Our results highlight a new source of investor disagreement, arising out of polarized reporting of corporate financial news, that generates trade among investors.

article.1. Introduction: Reporting on Amazon's quarterly earnings announcement on January 31, 2020, a New York Times article headline read ÔÇťAmazon Powers Forward With Hefty Quarterly ProfitÔÇŁ, while the Wall Street Journal headline said ÔÇťAmazon Misses $1 Trillion MilestoneÔÇŁ. Amazon is more politically aligned with the Democratic Party: in 2020, 85% of Amazon's employee and PAC campaign contributions supported political candidates from the Democratic Party.Motivated by this example, we use a broad sample of corporations to test if the difference in coverage between media sources is driven by politics, and whether such differential coverage generates disagreement among investors.Theory suggests that investor disagreement leads to trade (Varian, 1985; Milgrom and Stokey, 1982; Karpoff, 1986; Harris and Raviv, 1993), where disagreement may arise because of heterogeneous priors (Harris and Raviv, 1993; Kandel and Pearson, 1995) or differential information (Hong and Stein, 1999). Recent empirical evidence has shown that both sources of disagreement can matter but has mainly used investor-centric measures of information and beliefs (see for example Cookson and Niessner (2020); Cookson et al. (2023)). As financial media are a major component of information dissemination, we study whether the production of firm-specific financial news is affected by politics and if this is an important source of informational disagreement among investors.Using political polarization as the empirical setting, we study whether the opposing political ideologies of two major financial news sources, the Wall Street Journal and the New York Times, affects the reporting of corporate financial news, and if politics-induced disagreement in corporate news generates trade in a company's stock. Our main findings are as follows: First, we find evidence of political polarization in the coverage of corporate financial news. We show that newspapers use more positive language and are more likely to report good rather than bad news about politically aligned firms. These effects become more pronounced following the sale of the Wall Street Journal to the more conservative News Corporation. Second, we find that the greater the disagreement in the tone and content of corporate financial news between news sources, the higher the abnormal daily trading volume in the most politically extreme firms. Our results highlight a new source of investor disagreement that generates trade.The empirical literature on investor disagreement has studied the effect of differences in information and beliefs on investor disagreement and trade.1 For example, Diether et al. (2002) use dispersion in analyst forecasts to show that stocks with higher dispersion earn lower future returns. Cookson and Niessner (2020) use investor tweets to show that differences in both investor beliefs and information may generate disagreement, although within-group disagreement has a greater impact on trading volume. Identifying differences in beliefs using investor tweets, Cookson et al. (2023) show that investors sort into ÔÇťecho chambersÔÇŁ, which affects returns and volume, and Jiao et al. (2020) find that social media echo chambers increase volatility and turnover.We make two main contributions to this literature. First, we introduce a direct measure of information disagreement arising out of politics and show that this disagreement occurs in primary news sources, which is a necessary building block for the differential information sets of investors. Second, we show that the politics-induced disagreement in the reporting of financial news about firms affects trading volume. Importantly, our setting of political polarization satisfies the two conditions necessary for information differences to sustain investor disagreement and thereby generate trade: (1) polarization leads news sources to differ systematically in their coverage and, (2) people consume different news sources that match their political views.We also make the following unique contributions to the literature on polarization in the news, which has shown that readers seek out news sources that confirm their political views (Iyengar and Hahn, 2009; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2011; Bakshy et al., 2015).2 First, our paper provides novel evidence on the production side of news, shedding unique light into decisions about the intensive and extensive choices of coverage. Second, we contribute to our understanding of polarization by showing that the trend in media polarization extends beyond political topics into corporate financial news, which is expected to be objective (Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2006).Lastly, we contribute to the literature on financial news and the stock market.3 Unlike the coverage of political news (Mullainathan and Shleifer, 2005; Groseclose and Milyo, 2005; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2006, Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010; Boxell et al., 2020), corporate financial news is not expected to be covered with political slant because the core purpose of this news is to inform the financial decisions of readers. In fact, a large literature has studied the effect of financial news on stock markets without considering the political slant of this news.4 We add to this literature by showing the novel result that the same corporate financial news is covered differently based on the political alignment between the firm and the news source, which impacts trading behavior.To identify the impact of political polarization on financial news we need to first measure political alignment between the newspaper and firm, and second, distinguish political alignment from other newspaper and firm characteristics that may also affect coverage. To do this we compare three decades of articles published in the conservative Wall Street Journal (WSJ) and the liberal New York Times (NYT) covering financial news on the 100 largest listed firms in the United States. These are the two most widely circulated national newspapers with business news sections, and their editorial pages occupy opposite ends of the political spectrum (Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010).5 We use campaign contributions by firms to Democratic and Republican Party candidates to identify firm-level political alignment.6To isolate the effect of political alignment on coverage and trading from other firm and newspaper characteristics, we follow the methodology in Fracassi et al. (2016) and Kempf and Tsoutsoura (2021) and include interacted Firm ├Ś Time and Paper ├Ś Time fixed effects, which absorb time-varying firm and newspaper characteristics. We also control for article topic fixed-effects, firm-newspaper-specific advertising expenditures, firm size, age, and employment, and compare coverage of the same firm-specific news events.We observe differences in both the intensive and extensive margins of coverage based on the political alignment between the firm and the newspaper. On the intensive margin, we find that newspapers use more positive words and tone in articles about politically aligned firms. For example, the WSJ uses 15% more positive words than the NYT in articles about firms that donate only to Republican Party candidates compared to firms that donate only to Democratic Party candidates. On the extensive margin, we show that a newspaper is more likely to publish good rather than bad financial news and write longer articles about politically aligned firms.Additionally, we find that the Wall Street Journal is more likely to publish an article and use more positive tone about Republican-leaning firms, following its acquisition by the more conservative News Corporation. Using a sub-sample of articles written by journalists who switch jobs between the two newspapers, we find that journalists change their tone to reflect the ideology of the newspaper, which shows that the tone of coverage depends on the firm's political alignment with the newspaper, not the journalist.To identify the impact of politics-induced disagreement on trading we need to first measure disagreement in coverage, and second, distinguish the impact of disagreement in the news from that of newsworthy events. To measure disagreement, we compare articles written on the same day about the same firm in both newspapers, and use three measures of differential coverage. These include the absolute value of the difference in the tone of coverage; absolute value of the difference in positive words; and, cosine similarity, which measures similarity in the words used in the articles.To distinguish disagreement from omitted variables that may also affect volume, we study the differential impact of disagreement on the abnormal volume of the most politically extreme firms (top 20th percentile of campaign contributions to either political party) compared to less politically extreme firms. This mitigates the identification concern that news events rather than disagreement in coverage drives volume because such events are not more likely to occur for politically extreme firms. Lastly, we control for observable and unobservable, time-varying firm and news characteristics that may affect abnormal volume.We find that the greater the disagreement in coverage between the two newspapers, the greater the increase in abnormal trading volume for the most politically extreme firms. For example, comparing days with no difference in the tone of coverage to days with the maximum difference in tone, daily abnormal trading volume is 30% higher for a top political donor compared to a non-top donor. We observe similar results using the difference in positive words and cosine similarity between articles in both newspapers about the same firm. In addition, we show that these results are not driven by specific news topics or by the largest firms. For example, disagreement in the coverage of corporate earnings announcements has the same impact on abnormal volume as disagreement about other news topics. Overall our results show that there is political bias in the reporting of corporate financial news, and that this biased reporting influences individual investors' trading decisions.1.1. Related literatureIn addition to the literature discussed above, our paper is related to studies that examine the responses of firms and investors to political bias in the media. Luo et al. (2020) find that following the acquisition of Dow Jones by News Corporation, stock prices of Republican firms respond less to Dow Jones Newswires sentiment although they do not find an increase in bias, which they interpret as a ÔÇśfake newsÔÇÖ effect. Baloria and Heese (2018) assume that Fox News is politically biased against Democratic firms, and show that firms affiliated with the Democratic Party that are located in markets with Fox News channels suppress bad news, and Knill et al. (2019) show that firms led by Republican-leaning CEOs headquartered in regions where Fox News was introduced increased their investment expenditures during the Bush presidency. While these studies assume there is political slant in a single media outlet, we find strong evidence of political polarization in the coverage of corporate financial news across media outlets, and show that this can generate disagreement in the information sets of investors.Second, our paper is broadly related to studies that examine whether connections between firms and the media affect coverage. For example, Reuter and Zitzewitz (2006) and Gurun and Butler (2012) show that coverage may be correlated with advertising, and Dyck et al. (2008) find that public relations by an investment fund increased coverage of corporate governance violations by Russian firms. Our results are robust to controlling for advertising expenditures by firms in each newspaper.Third, our paper is related to the literature on the impact of financial news on markets. For example, Huberman and Regev (2001) and Tetlock (2011) observe that stock market returns respond to stale news; Barber and Odean (2008) find that individual investors buy stocks reported in the news; Fang and Peress (2009) show that media coverage reduces information frictions; Engelberg and Parsons (2011) find that local media coverage predicts local trading; Dougal et al. (2012) show that short-term returns can be predicted using fixed-effects for columnists at the WSJ; Garcia (2013) finds that the predictability of stock returns using news content is concentrated in recessions; Hillert et al. (2014) show that the media can exacerbate investor biases; Ben-Rephael et al. (2017) show that the impact of news on financial markets depends on the nature of the readership; and, Goldman et al. (2022) provide a theory of the informational role of financial media in selectively disseminating firm announcements. The above studies do not take into account political slant in the reporting of financial news about firms.Lastly, our paper contributes to the politics and finance literature that finds positive effects of political connections, campaign contributions, and lobbying on firm value (see, for example, Fisman, 2001; Faccio, 2006; Borisov et al., 2016; Akey, 2015), and documents the effect of political bias on economic expectations (Mian et al., 2017; Meeuwis et al., 2019; Kempf and Tsoutsoura, 2021; Cookson et al., 2020). For example, Mian et al. (2017) find that Republican party aligned households had more optimistic economic expectations following the 2016 election than Democratic party aligned households; Meeuwis et al. (2019) show that political party affiliation affected household investment behavior following the 2016 presidential election; Kempf and Tsoutsoura (2021) show that credit rating analysts behave in a partisan fashion based on the party of the president, which affects their broad economic outlook; and, Cookson et al. (2020) find that during the COVID-19 crisis Republican investors were more optimistic about stocks that had suffered the most. While these papers study how political biases affect the broad economic outlook of individuals, we study how political biases affect the production of information about a cross-section of firms and the impact on investors.
article.2. Data: Based on market capitalization in 2017, we conduct our analysis on the largest 100 publicly traded U.S. firms, with at least 10 articles that mention the firm published in either the Wall Street Journal or the New York Times between 1991 and 2016.7 We focus on these larger firms because they are likely to receive more news coverage. We choose the New York Times and Wall Street Journal for three reasons: First, these are the two most widely circulated national newspapers in the United States that are also important sources of business news; Second, their editorial positions occupy opposite ends of the political spectrum (Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010), which allows us to study the effects of political polarization on financial news coverage; Third, the finance literature has used these newspapers to study the effect of financial news on markets (Huberman and Regev, 2001; Tetlock, 2007; Dougal et al., 2012; Garcia, 2013).From Factiva we gather all articles from the print editions of the New York Times and the Wall Street Journal that mention any of the firms in our sample between 1991 and 2016. We collect the text of each article, section and page numbers, topic codes that classify articles, and the name of the journalist when identified. To clearly identify the content of the article with a specific firm, we focus on articles that only mention one firm.We use the standard Loughran and McDonald (2011) financial dictionary to classify the tone of a financial news article. We count the number of positive and negative words in each article to create our measures of tone. Specifically we measure the tone of an article using the following variables: Positive Words is equal to Positive Words/(Positive + Negative Words); and Tone is equal to (Positive-Negative Words)/(Positive + Negative Words). Both variables are expressed in percentages.To construct an additional direct measure of good and bad news we search news articles for sentences describing a firm's stock returns, share price, etc., and determine whether the sentence describes positive returns (e.g., ÔÇťshare prices roseÔÇŁ, ÔÇťprices soaredÔÇŁ, ÔÇťpositive returnÔÇŁ, etc.), or negative returns (e.g., ÔÇťstock price fellÔÇŁ, ÔÇťprices were downÔÇŁ, ÔÇťnegative returnÔÇŁ, etc.). We then aggregate the number of times a newspaper reports positive returns and negative returns for each stock during each 2-year election cycle, and create the variable %Good News as the number of articles that mention positive returns divided by the total number of articles mentioning either positive or negative returns about a stock in each newspaper.8 To reduce noise, we require a minimum of 1 mention per quarter, on average, during a given election cycle.To measure a firm's political alignment, we collect data on campaign contributions made by employees and Political Action Committees (PACs) of firms from the Center for Responsive Politics, which obtains the data from the Federal Election Commission. Donations are available for every two-year election cycle between 1990 and 2016. We aggregate donations to the firm level and construct the fraction of total campaign contributions by employees and firm-level PACs to Republican Party candidates in an election cycle (% Contributions to Republican Party), which is approximately equal to 1-%Contributions to Democratic Party since almost all firm contributions go to the two parties. We match campaign contributions data from the previous two-year election cycle for each firm to each year of the financial news data. Lastly, we obtain firm-level market data from CRSP and financial characteristics from Compustat.We obtain firm-newspaper advertising expenditures from Kantar Media. These data report advertising expenditures for each firm in each newspaper starting in 1995. We use these data to control for newspaper-specific advertising expenses by each firm. We also control for age (IPO year), size (market capitalization), and employment for each firm.To study the effect of disagreement on volume we focus on days on which there are the same number of articles about a firm in the two newspapers. We construct three disagreement measures based on differences in the tone and content of articles about the same firm across the two newspapers. Specifically, we use:Difference in Positive Wordsi,t=|(PositiveWordsPositive+NegativeWords)i,tWSJÔłĺ(PositiveWordsPositive+NegativeWords)i,tNYT|; andDifference in Tonei,t=|(PositiveWordsÔłĺNegativeWordsPositive+NegativeWords)i,tWSJÔłĺ(PositiveWordsÔłĺNegativeWordsPositiveWords+NegativeWords)i,tNYT|.On days with no articles in either newspaper these variables are equal to zero.The third measure of disagreement in the news is Cosine Dissimilarity, which captures the extent to which the words used by the two newspapers in articles about the same firm are different. For each day on which there is equal coverage in both newspapers, we create a measure of the dissimilarity of the words used in the articles between the two newspapers. For consistency with our other measure of disagreement in tone, we define Cosine Dissimilarity as 1-Cosine Similarity of the words across articles in the two newspapers. Cosine Similarity is the angle between the two newspapers' ÔÇťword vectorsÔÇŁ, which are n-dimensional numerical vectors where each element is the number of times a given word appears in the article(s) and n is the number of unique words across all articles.9 More formally, Cosine Similarity is the dot product of the two word vectors normalized by the vector length. When there is no overlap in words in the two newspapers, Cosine Dissimilarity is equal to 1. When both newspapers use exactly the same words, Cosine Dissimilarity is equal to 0.We define Abnormal Volume as the daily dollar trading volume of a firm divided by the average daily dollar trading volume over the previous 30 days. We define politically extreme firms with the variable Top Donor, which is an indicator variable that is equal to one if the firm is in the top 20th percentile of campaign contributions to either Republican or Democratic Party candidates in a given cycle.Table 1 reports summary statistics that describe the data. Table 1A describes firm characteristics including age, size, employment, advertising expenses, abnormal trading volume, and absolute returns. In Table 1B, we describe the campaign contributions of firms in our sample. Firms in our data donated about $980,000 on average per election cycle, of which 54% was to Republican Party candidates and 43% to Democratic Party candidates. The remaining contributions are to third party and independent candidates. Table 1C describes Positive Words, Tone and average article length for each newspaper. We also describe the three measures of disagreement in coverage between the two newspapers, which include Disagreement in Positive Words, Disagreement in Tone, and Cosine Dissimilarity.Table 1. article.3. Empirical strategy: 3.1. Coverage and political alignmentTone of CoverageWe first study whether political polarization affects newspaper coverage by comparing the tone of coverage of articles about each firm across the two newspapers based on the political alignment between the firm and the newspaper. We start with the following specification:(1)Tonei,j,t=╬▓1WSJj├Ś%ContributionstoRepublicanPartyi,t+╬▓2Xi,t+╬▒i,t+╬▒j,t+╬▒Topic+¤Ái,j,t, where i represents firms, j represents the news source (WSJ or NYT), and t represents article publication date. Our unit of observation is an article on a given day, the political variable is measured in the two-year election cycle associated with the article publication date, and the firm-level controls are annual. We use two measures of article tone: Positive Words/(Positive + Negative Words), which captures positive tone and Tone = (Positive Ôłĺ Negative Words)/ (Positive + Negative Words), which captures the difference in positive and negative words. WSJ is an indicator variable that is equal to one if an article appears in the Wall Street Journal and zero if it appears in the New York Times. % Contributions to Republican Party is the percentage of campaign contributions to Republican Party candidates in a given election cycle. In the most restrictive specifications we include ╬▒i,t and ╬▒j,t, which represent interacted firm-quarter-year and newspaper-quarter-year fixed effects, respectively. Lastly, in robustness checks we include annual firm-level age, market capitalization, and employment (Xi,t). We report standard errors that are double clustered at the firm and quarter-year level.Our key parameter of interest is ╬▓1, which captures how the Wall Street Journal covers firms that donate more to Republican Party candidates compared to the New York Times. If political polarization affects financial news coverage, then we expect ╬▓1 to be positive.To identify the impact of political polarization on financial news we need to address the concern that political alignment between the firm and the newspaper may be correlated with unobservable newspaper or firm-specific factors that also affect the tone of coverage. For example, the two newspapers may cover certain industries differently, report on different firm-specific events and topics, or write more positively about firms that advertise in the paper. Firms may also be subject to idiosyncratic shocks that lead to differences in coverage that are unrelated to their political alignment with the newspaper.In specification (1), we address these identification concerns using higher-order fixed effects. We include firm-quarter fixed effects (╬▒i,t) to compare coverage of the same firm at the same time between the two papers, and newspaper-quarter (╬▒j,t) fixed effects to control for newspaper-specific idiosyncratic shocks that may drive changes in coverage over time. We also include topic fixed effects, using two-digit topic codes from Factiva, to ensure coverage of similar topics across the papers. Note that by comparing coverage between the two newspapers of the same firm, we address the concern that a firm's politics is correlated with factors such as performance, which may affect how it is covered.In additional analysis, we use the acquisition of the Wall Street Journal by the more politically conservative News Corporation as a shock to the ideology of the newspaper that is exogenous to firm characteristics, and compare coverage following the acquisition. To ensure that the results are not driven by a few ideological journalists, we also consider a sub-sample of articles written by journalists who switch jobs between the two papers and control for journalist fixed effects. In robustness checks we compare coverage of the same firm-specific events using a sub-sample of articles covering the same firm on the same day in both papers; and, interact the WSJ indicator with firm-level age, size, and employment to further control for omitted variable bias.Coverage of Good versus Bad NewsTo study whether newspapers are more likely to cover good versus bad news about politically aligned firms, we consider a sub-sample of articles that mention the firm's stock returns and estimate the following model:(2)%GoodNewsi,j,t=╬▓1WSJj├Ś%ContributionstoRepublicanPartyi,t+╬▓2Xi,t+╬▒i,t+╬▒j,t+¤Ái,j,t where i denotes the firm, j the newspaper, and t represents election cycle. % Good News is the number of articles that mention positive returns divided by the total number of articles mentioning either positive or negative returns about a firm in each newspaper in a given election cycle. We aggregate the data at the 2-year election cycle in this specification to get a sufficiently large sample of news articles that mention positive or negative stock market returns. The remaining variables are described under specification (1).The key parameter of interest is ╬▓1, which captures whether newspapers write more articles that mention positive returns about politically aligned firms. Our identification strategy is similar to specification (1) and compares coverage of the same firm between the two newspapers, and includes firm-cycle-year (╬▒i,t) and newspaper-cycle-year (╬▒j,t) fixed effects in the most restrictive specifications. We report standard errors that are double clustered at the firm and election cycle level.Likelihood of CoverageWe study coverage along the extensive margin by examining if political alignment between the newspaper and the firm affects the likelihood of coverage. Specifically, we estimate the conditional probability that an article in our data appears in a given newspaper, with the following linear probability specification:(3)Pr(Coveragei,j,t=1)=╬▓1%ContributionstoRepublicanPartyi,t+╬▓2Xi,t+╬▒i+╬▒t+╬▒Topic+¤Ái,j,t where i represents firms, j represents the news source (WSJ or NYT), and t represents article publication date. Coverage is equal to 1 if an article about firm i is in the Wall Street Journal, and zero if it is in the New York Times. The remaining variables are described under specification (1). Our parameter of interest is ╬▓1, which captures the differential likelihood that an article in our data appears in the politically aligned paper. To address omitted variable bias, we control for firm, quarter, and two-digit topic fixed effects.10 We report standard errors that are double clustered at the firm and quarter-year level.3.2. Volume and disagreementTo study the effect of politics-induced disagreement on trading volume, we use the daily trading volume of firms for the entire sample period. We restrict the sample to days with the same number of articles in each newspaper in order to ensure equal news coverage between the two news sources. Our baseline specification is below:(4)AbnormalVolumei,t=╬▓1Disagreementi,t├ŚTopDonori,t+╬▓2Xi,t+╬▒i,t+¤Ái,j,t where i refers to firms and t the day. Abnormal Volumei,t is the dollar trading volume of firm i on day t divided by the average daily dollar trading volume for the same stock over the previous 30 days.11 We use three measures of Disagreement: Difference in Positive Wordsi,t is the absolute value of the scaled difference in positive words in the articles about firm i on date t between the WSJ and the NYT; Difference in Tonei,t is the absolute value of the scaled difference in tone between the two newspapers; and, Cosine Dissimilarityi,t, which captures the extent to which the same words are used by both newspapers in articles about firm i on date t.Our measure of a firm's politics is Top Donor, which is an indicator variable equal to one if the firm is in the top 20th percentile of campaign contributions in our sample, to either Republican or Democratic Party candidates in a given cycle. Xi,t includes stock and news characteristics that may affect abnormal volume including daily Absolute Returns, Lagged Absolute Returns, and Lagged Abnormal Volume at the firm level, Total Articles, which is the total number of articles published in both newspapers on a given day about a firm, and Total Words, which is article length. Lastly, in the most restrictive specifications we control for firm-quarter-year fixed effects (╬▒j,t), which absorb time-varying firm-specific factors that may affect volume, and report standard errors that are double clustered at the firm and quarter-year level.Our main parameter of interest is ╬▓1, which is the coefficient of the interaction between the disagreement measure and Top Donor. If polarization leads to information segregation, we hypothesize that ╬▓1>0 as politics generated disagreement in financial news coverage should lead to higher abnormal trading volume for more politically extreme firms compared to non-extreme firms.We address the main identification concern that omitted variables (such as newsworthy events) that may drive abnormal volume are correlated with the reporting of news in three ways. First, we focus on the interaction between Top Donor and our measures of disagreement, which captures the impact of disagreement on politically extreme compared to less extreme firms. This mitigates the concern that news rather than disagreement in coverage drives volume because newsworthy events are unlikely to occur only for more politically extreme firms.12 Second, we control for unobservable, firm-specific idiosyncratic factors that may drive abnormal volume with interacted firm-quarter fixed effects. As mentioned, our specifications also control for observable firm and news characteristics that may affect abnormal volume. Lastly, we use a sample of news days with equal coverage in both newspapers.
article.4. Results: 4.1. Tone of financial news coverageWe start by studying whether political polarization affects newspaper coverage by comparing the tone of coverage based on political alignment between the firm and newspaper. The results from estimating specification (1) are described in Table 2.Table 2. Abnormal daily trading volume and political disagreementOur results show that political polarization can generate disagreement in the reporting of corporate financial news. Since polarization causes individuals to seek out news sources that match their views (Iyengar and Hahn, 2009; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2011), this may lead to sustained differences in the information sets of investors. Theory suggests that disagreement among investors is central to trading in financial markets (Milgrom and Stokey, 1982; Karpoff, 1986; Harris and Raviv, 1993). article.7. Conclusion: Comparing 25 years of corporate financial news articles in the liberal New York Times to the conservative Wall Street Journal, we find evidence of political polarization in the reporting of this news. Specifically, we find that the tone and content of coverage of firm-specific financial news varies based on the political alignment between the firm and newspaper.Based on this finding, we directly measure disagreement between primary news sources in the production of corporate financial news, and show that this generates disagreement among investors. In particular, we find that for the most politically extreme firms, disagreement in the tone and content of coverage of corporate financial news leads to higher abnormal trading volume. Since political polarization implies that market participants may be exposed to different news, this empirical setting satisfies the conditions necessary for information differences to sustain investor disagreement.
